data.title: Nestlé: New CEO's Strategic Pivot Strengthens The Buy Case
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY) (OTCPK:NSRGF), I highlighted a mix of multi-year lows combined with near-term catalysts, high debt and unsustainable buybacks, and a CEO who's been recently fired.

Now, however, the new CEO has introduced updated guidance and strategy, revealing a major change to the company's cost transformation plan back in October, which can potentially lead to an even better turnaround opportunity going forward.

Internal Developments

Nestlé's Q3 report was relatively solid, with a 4.3% organic growth thanks to an improvement in real internal growth and pricing increases, marking an improvement compared to the previous two quarters. Meanwhile, the company expects to generate at least CHF 8 billion ($9.95 billion) of free cash flow this year, which is not terrible given the consumer weakness and the company's initiatives.

As mentioned, Nestlé's new CEO came with an updated plan to simplify and transform the business, reinvesting and aiming to deliver accelerated growth. The plan is estimated to generate about CHF 3 billion (~$3.73 billion) in total "fuel for growth" run-rate savings by the end of 2027, with probably the biggest headline being their plan to fire ~12,000 white-collar jobs and ~4,000 manufacturing ones, representing a roughly 6% workforce reduction over these two years.

I'll also note that they expect CHF 1 billion ($1.24 billion) in annual cost savings from the white-collar job cuts, with a one-time restructuring cost of approximately twice the expected annual savings, as the CFO mentioned during the Q3 Sales Call:

These additional savings will be reinvested, more fuel for driving growth. For the headcount reductions, there will be a restructuring cost of about 2x the annual savings, so expected to be around CHF 2 billion. In short, we are increasing efficiency and reducing complexity as we accelerate business transformation.

Still, Nestlé now sees improved 2025 guidance, while the medium term is still expected to deliver over 4% organic sales growth in normal market conditions at an underlying trading operating margin of over 17%. I'd also point out that Nestlé has the advantage of owning some of the most popular brands on the planet, which should make it easier to turn the business around or develop new product lines/markets compared to building it from scratch.

As they say, they remain committed to investing in the medium term despite the ongoing macro and consumer uncertainties, which could make a potential turnaround even better once things improve, which the new CEO also talked about during the Q3 Sales Call:

We have strong foundations to build on. But let's be clear, we have a lot of work to do. I am very focused on how we move faster with our transformation to accelerate our growth momentum. And the action we are now taking will secure Nestlé's future as a leader in our industry. We have been making good financial progress with a strong Q3. Our investments in growth are starting to show results. We are determined to deliver on our commitments, and I am confirming our full year 2025 guidance. We are moving in the right direction. Now we need to move faster.

Macro Developments

As mentioned, US rate cuts will play a very important role even for a global giant like Nestlé. These will be very important for the company from many points of view, including reducing their interest and making new debt cheaper, potentially fueling accelerated investments, all while also reducing the theoretical risk-free rate over time and potentially boosting the demand for their stock (which pays a decent ~3.73% dividend yield now) and improving this source of external capital as well.

Also very important is the effect on the discretionary budgets of the company's consumers, as rate hikes have similar effects on companies and people, limiting their budgets by increasing the cost of debt.

These can have important implications for global trade as well, potentially being a key catalyst for Nestlé's turnaround, which could be boosted by their accelerated investments and higher growth capital thanks to the layoffs. I believe tariffs cooling down could also offer an opportunity here, as I don't see them as a long-term goal but rather a near-term tool for leverage in negotiating wider deals, similar to the US-EU LNG deal, the recent advancements with China, or the reductions to Brazilian tariffs.

As a note, Nestlé said that their business is largely immune to tariffs since they "produce where they sell," but that's not going to be the case. Realistically, no company is really immune to the potential negative effects of inflation, budgets, spending, commodity prices (e.g., coffee, which the CEO called "an incredible growth opportunity" when referring to their cold coffee business), and others that would influence the company's volumes, margins, or pricing mix.

Still, these tariffs likely improving at roughly the same time as rate cuts - which could be more likely after Powell's term ends in May 2026 and Trump will probably name a more obedient Fed Chair this time - could be important catalysts to keep an eye on.

I'll note again that the Fed Chair is just one out of twelve votes in the Federal Open Market Committee, but we've seen especially lately that this person is a very important spokesperson, as their views and remarks translate into a ton of influence on the market, so Trump's new choice here could make significant waves in the market.

Risks to the Thesis

However, don't expect these improvements/recovery to come overnight. This can take years to be reflected in the company's financials or benefit their consumers, especially after the US Government recently went through its longest shutdown in history, making the near-term future of US rate cuts uncertain, although some Fed officials signaled they see the possibility of a December cut - but we should know for sure soon.

So, even though I like the new CEO's accelerated cost savings, the potential of their brands, and ramped-up investments, that doesn't guarantee Nestlé's success, and execution risk combined with political pressure or even social ones (as mentioned in the previous analysis) can easily put more pressure on the business and its stock.

And these are on top of tariffs that can easily affect the company's consumers, potentially delaying their turnaround significantly and emphasizing the company's dependency on the global economy - which the company also mentioned several times, citing macro risks and uncertainties during their opening statements before and once again during the latest guidance discussions.

Updated Valuation

Starting with Nestlé's expected FCF of at least CHF 8 billion ($9.95 billion), I'll assume it can grow to $12 billion in 2027 (below expected savings and slightly above their 5-year average; remaining flat in 2026), with a 6.5% CAGR until year 5 (an upgrade thanks to the accelerated cost savings and investments), followed by a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate to account for a slight premium on inflation given their brands and investments. I'll go for a 6% WACC (assumptions: ~6.5% cost of equity, ~3.8% cost of debt), a net debt of ~$75.74 billion, and $278.55 million in non-controlling interests, with 2.57 billion shares outstanding. This gets us to an enterprise value of $380.65 billion, an equity value of $304.63 billion, and an intrinsic value of $118.53 per share.

Conclusion

Overall, Nestlé still offers a good opportunity for solid and potentially stable long-term income, with some of the most popular brands on the planet, a new CEO, and solid turnaround potential. Risks remain, as the turnaround isn't guaranteed to work while macro pressure can linger for years ahead, although their more aggressive approach can also pay out and result in a better opportunity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
